food_subgroup: Crustaceans — cooked shrimp / prawns with spiced sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for spicy stir-fried shrimp; closest parent 'shrimp food product' (FOODON_03302757) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW (Shrimps and prawns) is the closest parent; no specific code for spicy prepared shrimp dish exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227144002 represents shrimp as a substance; no specific concept for spicy shrimp dish exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of spicy shrimp as a shared dish in a Chinese restaurant is approximately 200 g, representing a single-person portion of a multi-dish meal.

nutrient_source.title: Shrimp, cooked, moist heat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15152); adapted with spice and sauce additions estimated from USDA Foundation Foods condiment data
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base shrimp macros derived from USDA FoodData Central cooked shrimp (FDC ID 175180). Energy, protein, and fat adjusted upward to account for stir-frying in oil (~5 g oil per 100 g dish) and spiced sauce (soy sauce, chilli, garlic). Carbohydrate and sodium elevated to reflect sauce contribution. Values represent a composite estimate for the finished spicy shrimp dish per 100 g edible portion.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — shrimp and seafood dishes; analogous estimate based on cooked shrimp and low-carbohydrate stir-fried seafood
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for spicy shrimp as a composite dish. Shrimp and similar low-carbohydrate seafood dishes consistently show GI values in the range of 5–20 in the University of Sydney GI Database due to negligible starch content. A GI of 15 is assigned as a conservative analogous estimate for this high-protein, low-carbohydrate stir-fried shrimp dish. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (15 × 4.5 × 2.0) / 100 = 1.35, rounded to 1.4.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and seasoning (~520 mg per 100 g; ~1040 mg per 200 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced-sodium preparation, as a single 200 g serving may contribute approximately 45% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (GI ~15) and low carbohydrate content (~4.5 g per 100 g) with high protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: Spicy shrimp is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as its low GI and high protein content support glycemic control; however, the sodium-rich sauce warrants attention in those with comorbid hypertension.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated dietary cholesterol (~152 mg per 100 g) and high sodium, partially offset by beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from shrimp
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should moderate consumption due to high cholesterol and sodium content, while recognising that shrimp provides beneficial omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids and lean protein that may support cardiovascular health when consumed in controlled portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spicy Shrimp (香辣虾, Xiāng là xiā) is a popular Chinese stir-fried seafood dish consisting of whole or shelled shrimp cooked in a wok over high heat with chilli peppers, garlic, ginger, soy sauce, and cooking oil. The dish is characterised by a bold, fiery flavour profile with savoury umami notes from the soy sauce, aromatic heat from fresh or dried chillies, and a slightly caramelised exterior on the shrimp from wok-frying. The texture is firm and succulent. In Singapore, this dish is commonly served as part of a shared Chinese meal alongside rice and vegetables. Nutritionally, spicy shrimp is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate dish with moderate fat content primarily from cooking oil. It is rich in selenium, vitamin B12, phosphorus, and iodine from the shrimp. The sodium content is elevated due to soy sauce and seasoning, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is very low, making it appropriate for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is naturally gluten-free if tamari is substituted for soy sauce, and is dairy-free and halal-compliant when prepared without pork-derived ingredients.
common_user_queries: How many calories in spicy shrimp?
Is spicy shrimp good for diabetics?
Can I eat spicy shrimp with high blood pressure?
Is spicy shrimp high in cholesterol?

confidence_reason: No exact match for spicy shrimp as a composite dish was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central cooked shrimp data with estimated adjustments for stir-frying oil and soy sauce seasoning. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on comparable low-carbohydrate seafood dishes. Confidence is medium rather than low because the base ingredient data is from a high-quality approved source and the adaptations are methodologically defensible.
notes: 1. Food group corrected from input value 'Grains and staples' to 'Fish and seafood' as shrimp is a crustacean seafood product. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central cooked shrimp source entry. 3. Trans fat is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for cooked shrimp; naturally zero in this seafood dish. 4. Nutrient values represent a composite estimate for the finished dish per 100 g edible portion, accounting for stir-frying oil (~5 g per 100 g) and soy sauce seasoning. 5. Sodium is elevated (~520 mg/100 g) due to soy sauce; actual restaurant values may vary. 6. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 7. The input cuisine_tags included 'Noodle' which is not applicable to this dish and has been disregarded. 8. This dish is served at Bei Jiang Restaurant (北疆饭店), a Northern Chinese restaurant in Singapore, suggesting the preparation may reflect Northern Chinese spice profiles.
